_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The ICAP Status Report 2026 documents a global regulatory landscape in which 41 emissions trading systems are now in force, together covering 26% of global GHG emissions and jurisdictions representing 63% of global GDP. As a freight forwarder operating across air, road, and ocean modes with concentrated activity on Americas–Europe–Asia lanes, the workspace sits within the compliance chain as a transport-services intermediary and, in certain jurisdictions, a logistics operator or shipping agent — roles that may carry direct or indirect ETS obligations depending on how each scheme defines its regulated entity.

For the EU ETS (ETS 1), the regulated entity for aviation is the aircraft operator; for maritime, the shipping company as defined in EU MRV Regulation. The workspace is not itself a regulated entity unless it operates aircraft or vessels. However, it is a primary conduit through which ETS compliance costs flow downstream: airlines and ocean carriers pass carbon surcharges to forwarders and shippers, making the workspace a critical node for cost transparency, contract negotiation, and client-side disclosure. The workspace should obtain verified documentation of carrier ETS compliance costs and assess whether carrier surcharge methodologies are consistent with actual allowance costs.

For the UK ETS, the regulated entity for aviation is the aircraft operator; for maritime (from 1 July 2026), the ship operator. The workspace's UK-touching lanes (UK–EEA air freight; UK domestic maritime consolidation) face structural cost changes: aviation free allocation is fully phased out from January 2026 and domestic maritime enters the scheme in July 2026. The workspace should confirm with each carrier whether it is a UK ETS-registered operator, and capture that information in carrier-onboarding records.

For Japan's GX-ETS, the mandatory scheme from April 2026 covers companies emitting ≥100,000 tCO₂/year in power, industry, transport, and aviation. The workspace's Japanese carrier partners operating domestic routes may be covered. Confirmation of their regulated status — and the benchmark formulas applied to their allocation — requires legal and commercial verification with each Japan-based air and road carrier.

Role placement requiring legal confirmation: (1) Whether the workspace acts as a 'consignor' or 'principal' in any jurisdiction that treats freight forwarders as regulated entities under transport-sector ETS rules; (2) Whether the workspace's own road-transport emissions (company-controlled road freight) fall within any national or sub-national ETS road-transport scope expansion under consideration in the EU ETS 2 (buildings and road transport, delayed to 2028) or German nEHS.

*Source: ICAP Status Report 2026: Emissions Trading Worldwide, International Carbon Action Partnership, 14 April 2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/publications/emissions-trading-worldwide-icap-status-report-2026. UK ETS Factsheet, ICAP ETS Map, International Carbon Action Partnership (updated 2026). https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/uk-emissions-trading-scheme-uk-ets. Japan GX-ETS Factsheet, ICAP ETS Map, International Carbon Action Partnership (updated 2026). https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets/japan-gx-ets.*
